text: C-84 - Reactors-Special Features of Aircraft Reactors

This quarterly progress report of the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Project at ORNL records the technical progress of the research on circulating-fuel reactors and other ANP research at the Laboratory under its Contract W-7405-eng-26.
The report is divided into five major parts: 1.
Aircraft Reactor Engineering, 2.
Heat Transfer and Physical Properties, Radiation Damage, Fuel Recovery and Reprocessing, and Critical Experiments, and 5.
The ANP Project is comprised of about 550 technical and scientific personnel engaged in many phases of research directed toward the achievement of nuclear propulsion of aircraft.

The present operating plan for the ART anticipates that the reactor will be cycled from the idle condition $(1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ isothermal) to full power some 30 times.
One cycle consists of 8 hr at idle and 16 hr at full power.

In passing from the idle condition to full power the temperature distribution in the core shells will change from a uniform profile to a linear distribution, with a maximum gradient of about $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ through the wall.
The test cycle selected was 1 hr at power and 1 hr isothermal at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
At the power condition the shell is exposed to a maximum temperature gradient of $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ through the wall.

In studying the relaxation data for Inconel, it was found that because of the very rapid decay of the stress it was not necessary to design the core-shell test for the 8- to 16-hr cycle time planned for the reactor.
The 2-hr cycle time was found to be sufficient to develop a substantial fraction of the plastic deformation expected in the actual shells.

On this basis it was argued that the cycle times for the model test could be reduced to the 1-hr intervals being used, since approximately 70 to $90\%$ of the total strain developed in 8 to 16 hr could be achieved in 1 hr.

The evidence from these and other quenched samples from this system indicates that there is a metastable compound in the $\mathsf{LiF - UF_4}$ system whose formula is $3\mathsf{LiF - UF}_4$ In the quenched samples the compound has the appearance of quench growth.

The crystals are sufficiently well formed to give a good uniaxial-negative interference figure and a refractive index of approximately 1.514, but they have the characteristic mottled texture and wavy extinction of quench
Large quenched samples ( $\sim 10$ mg) contained $3\mathrm{LiF}\cdot \mathrm{UF}_4$ as quench growth.

Medium-sized ( $\sim 5\mathrm{mg}$ ) or very small samples (2 to $3\mathrm{mg}$ ) quenched in platinum tubes allowed the liquid to freeze to material with a glassy appearance and isotropic optical character, but even in these samples x-ray analysis showed the glassy material to be largely $3\mathrm{LiF}\cdot \mathrm{UF}_4$ .

There is no observable difference, optically or by x-ray pattern, in the $3\mathrm{LiF}\cdot \mathrm{UF}_4$ which occurs below the liquidus and that from quenches which were attempted well above the accepted liquidus.
Under no conditions has $3\mathrm{LiF}\cdot \mathrm{UF}_4$ been observed as homogeneous, well-crystallized material free of quench growth texture.

The inconsistency of the appearance, petrographically, of the $3\mathrm{LiF}\cdot \mathrm{UF}_4$ which occurs as a glass (isotropic, no optic figures) and that which occurs as quench growth, with texture gradation, is evidence of the metastability of the compound.

The join $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2 - 2\text{NaF} \cdot \text{LiF} \cdot 2\text{BeF}_2$ was established as a quasi-binary system by the examination of equilibrated and quenched samples at 38 mole $\% \text{BeF}_2 - 35$ mole $\% \text{LiF} - 27$ mole $\% \text{NaF}$ .
The liquidus is $334^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the primary phase is $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$ .

An extrapolation places the binary eutectic at 38.5 mole $\% \text{BeF}_2 - 30.5$ mole $\% \text{LiF} - 31$ mole $\% \text{NaF}$ .

The join is not a true binary system because the primary phase field of LiF overhangs the compound $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$ very slightly. $^{13}$ The triangle LiF- $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2 - 2\text{NaF} \cdot \text{LiF} \cdot 2\text{BeF}_2$ is a compatibility triangle; no phases other than the three mentioned have been found in samples within the triangle.

Examinations of quenches of a composition containing 50 mole $\% \text{BeF}_2 - 38$ mole $\% \text{LiF} - 12$ mole $\% \text{NaF}$ , which is on the join $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2 - 3\text{TC}$ , show that the liquidus is above $354^{\circ}\text{C}$ and that the primary phase is $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$ .
The solidus is $280^{\circ}\text{C}$ , and the secondary phase is the ternary compound TC.
The absence of a third phase indicates that the composition is on or near the binary system $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2 - 3\text{TC}$ .
At the composition 50 mole $\% \text{BeF}_2 - 12$ mole $\% \text{LiF} - 38$ mole $\% \text{NaF}$ the liquidus is $344^{\circ}\text{C}$ , and the primary phase is NaF-BeF $_2$ .
Below the solidus ( $280^{\circ}\text{C}$ ) the three phases NaF-BeF $_2$ , $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$ , and the ternary phase TC coexist.

At the composition 50 mole $\% \text{BeF}_2 - 25$ mole $\% \text{LiF} - 25$ mole $\% \text{NaF}$ the liquidus was found to be $276^{\circ}\text{C}$ , with $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$ as the primary phase.
The same three phases, NaF-BeF $_2$ , $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$ , and the ternary phase TC, were found to be present below the solidus ( $271^{\circ}\text{C}$ ).
The results of these experiments show that $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$ , NaF-BeF $_2$ , and the ternary phase TC form a compatibility triangle.

However, the join $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2 - 2\text{NaF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$ is not a true binary system, even if the slight overhang of $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$ by the primary phase field of LiF is disregarded.

Quenching experiments with the composition 45 mole $\% \text{BeF}_2 - 20$ mole $\% \text{LiF} - 35$ mole $\% \text{NaF}$ , which is on the join, show that, down to $300^{\circ}\text{C}$ , $2\text{LiF} \cdot \text{BeF}_2$
and $2\mathrm{NaF}\cdot \mathrm{LiF}\cdot 2\mathrm{BeF}_2$ are present.
Thus the primary phase field of $2\mathrm{NaF}\cdot \mathrm{LiF}\cdot 2\mathrm{BeF}_2$ cuts across the join.
There must be a peritectic point below $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the triangle $2\mathrm{LiF}\cdot \mathrm{BeF}_2\cdot \mathrm{NaF}\cdot \mathrm{BeF}_2$ .
TC, where $2\mathrm{LiF}\cdot \mathrm{BeF}_2$ , $2\mathrm{NaF}\cdot \mathrm{LiF}\cdot 2\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , $2\mathrm{NaF}\cdot \mathrm{BeF}_2$ are in equilibrium.

Cooling curves obtained with compositions within the triangle NaF-RbF-3RbF-BeF $_2$ , previously reported to comprise a compatibility triangle, $^{14}$ indicated the existence of a ternary eutectic having a melting point of approximately $625^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Additional data will be required in order to determine the eutectic composition, but the available data show that it will be close to the NaF-RbF eutectic (74 mole % RbF, melting point $675^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

An unidentified crystalline phase, believed to be a ternary compound, has been observed in a number of slowly cooled melts containing 45 to 60 mole % BeF $_2$ ; this phase is usually associated with an isotropic phase believed to be glass.
The cooling curves for the mixture NaF-RbF-BeF $_2$ (35-15-50 mole %) showed only one thermal effect at about $240^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the cooled melt was found to be predominantly glass.

The occurrence of glass in ternary mixtures having BeF $_2$ concentrations that produce only crystalline compounds in the corresponding binary alkali fluoride-beryllium fluoride systems was noted previously in the NaF-KF-BeF $_2$ system. $^{15}$ This interesting phenomenon may be due either to the difference in ion sizes of the alkali ions involved or to the lowering of solidus temperatures in the ternary system to the point where the high viscosity of the melt inhibits crystallization.

There is also another compound, tentatively assigned the formula $7\mathrm{KCl}\cdot 6\mathrm{ZrCl}_4,$ which appears to melt incongruity to give $2\mathrm{KCl}\cdot \mathrm{ZrCl}_4$ and liquid at $565\pm 5^{\circ}C.$ The choice of the compound formula was based partly upon thermal analysis data, which indicate that the compound contains less than 50 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrCl}_4,$ and partly upon the results of petrographic examination of slowly cooled and quenched samples in this region.
Thermal effects on cooling curves at temperatures ranging from about 218 to $225^{\circ}C$ were noted with compositions containing more than $33\frac{1}{3}$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrCl}_4$

Since this is reasonably close to the reported $18$ melting point of the eutectic at 65 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrCl}_4$ $(234.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , the appearance of the thermal effect at a temperature close to this value with compositions containing less than 50 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrCl}_4$ might be taken as an indication of incomplete reaction of liquid with solid $2\mathrm{KCl} \cdot \mathrm{ZrCl}_4$ at the peritectic point.

However, the magnitude of the thermal effect on cooling curves, coupled with the fact that no liquid was visible in mixtures containing less than 50 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrCl}_4$ at temperatures below $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , indicates the likelihood that a solid phase transition occurs at a temperature so close to the solidus temperature that the two effects cannot be differentiated either by thermal analysis or differential thermal analysis.

Petrographic examination of a quenched sample containing 55 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrCl}_4$ showed that, above $237^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , well-crystallized phases were present but that at $219^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and lower temperatures the samples contained only microcrystalline aggregates.

Additional measurements of the surface tensions of NaF-ZrF $_4$ (53-47 mole %) mixtures have been made by using the sessile-drop technique, previously described. $^{3,4}$ Recent experiments at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ tend to substantiate the results reported previously.
Some data have been obtained at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and a preliminary experiment at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ has been run.
The data accumulated to date are summarized in Table 2.3.1.
Data from the previous report $^{4}$ are included and corrected by the use of more precise densities calculated from the equation

The "best" values for the surface tension of these mixtures are $127 \pm 6$ dynes/cm at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $119 \pm 4$ dynes/cm at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , where the error range is the root-mean-square deviation.
It should be noted that there appears to be a small difference (within experimental error) between the data obtained in helium and in hydrogen atmospheres at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The average surface tension in helium is 128 dynes/cm, while the average in hydrogen is 122 dynes/cm.
The first evidence of a large change of surface tension with composition is shown by the data at $800^{\circ}C$ .

In the previous report<sup>5</sup> the penetration of APC graphite by NaF-ZrF<sub>4</sub> (53-47 mole %) mixtures was described.
This behavior is surprising in view of the nonwetting properties of this mixture on C-18 graphite, as evidenced by the surface-tension measurements reported here.
Visual observation had previously indicated that melts containing $4\%$ , or more, $\mathsf{U}\mathsf{O}_2$ would penetrate graphite.

This observation was borne out by preliminary observations of the surface tension: No values for the surface tensions are available as yet, but the preliminary experiments have shown that, even though $\mathsf{U}\mathsf{F}_4$ with about $1\% \mathsf{U}\mathsf{O}_2$ did not immediately wet C-18 or APC graphite, after a period of time, during which small amounts of air leaked into the system, the contact angle receded and the $\mathsf{U}\mathsf{F}_4\cdot \mathsf{U}\mathsf{O}_2$ melt started to wet the plaques.
